sections.paragraph:
ἰατρός, οῦ, ὁ (s. ἰάομαι; Hom.+)

① one who undertakes the cure of physical ailments, physician Mt 9:12; Mk 2:17; Lk 5:31 (cp. on these pass. Plut., Mor. 230f, Phocion 746 [10, 5]; Stob., Floril. III p. 462, 14 H. οὐδὲ γὰρ ἰατρὸς ὑγιείας ὢν ποιητικὸς ἐν τοῖς ὑγιαίνουσι τὴν διατριβὴν ποιεῖται=no physician who can produce cures wastes time among the healthy); Ox 1 recto, 9–14 (ASyn. 33, 85, s. GTh 31; cp. Dio Chrys. 8 [9], 4 νοσοῦντες ἐπιδημοῦντος ἰατροῦ μὴ προσῄεσαν said in irony, of sick people unwilling to consult a resident physician); Mk 5:26 (Sb 8266, 13ff [161/160 b.c.] when physicians refuse to help, the god Amenothis intervenes with a miracle). ἰατροῖς προσαναλίσκειν ὅλον τὸν βίον spend all of one’s money on physicians Lk 8:43 v.l. (PStras 73, 18f, a physician’s fee of 20 drachmas; Diod S 32, 11, 3 a physician διπλοῦν ἀπῄτει τὸν μισθόν. But some physicians are honored for accepting no remuneration, s. FKudlien, in Sozialmassnahmen und Fürsorge, ed. HKloft, ’88, 90–92; s. also Danker, Benefactor, nos. 1–4 for positive view). Given as the profession of one named Luke Col 4:14 (Heraclid. Pont., Fgm. 118 W. Ἀσκληπιάδης ὁ ἰ.; Strabo 10, 5, 6 p. 486 Ἐρασίστρατος ὁ ἰ.; Sb 8327 [ins II a.d.] Ἀπολλώνιος ἰατρός). In a proverb (s. Jülicher, Gleichn. 172f; EKlostermann and FHauck ad loc.) ἰατρὲ θεράπευσον σεαυτόν physician, heal yourself Lk 4:23 (Eur., Fgm. 1086 Nauck2 ἄλλων ἰατρὸς αὐτὸς ἕλκεσιν βρύων. Aesop, Fab. 289 P.=H. 78 and 78b=Babr. 120 πῶς ἄλλον ἰήσῃ, ὸ̔ς σαυτὸν μὴ σῴζεις).—Papias (3:2); AcPl Ha 5, 34. For IEph 7:2 s. 2.

② one who undertakes the healing of supra-physical maladies, physician (of the soul) (Diog. L. 3, 45 an epigram calls Plato the ἰητὴρ ψυχῆς; schol. on Pla. 227a ὁ Σωκράτης ἰατρὸς περὶ ψυχήν; Diod S 34+35 Fgm. 17, 1 τῆς λύπης ὁ κάλλιστος ἰατρὸς χρόνος; Philo, Spec. Leg. 2, 31 ἰ. ἁμαρτημάτων) of God (s. DRahnenführer, Das Testament des Hiob, ZNW 62, ’71, 76; Aristoph., Av. 584 and Lycophron 1207; 1377 of Apollo; Simplicius in Epict. p. 41, 51 God as ἰατρός; Ael. Aristid. 47, 57 K.=23 p. 459 D.: Asclepius as ἀληθινὸς ἰατρός) Dg 9:6. Of Jesus Christ ἰ. σαρκικὸς καὶ πνευματικός physician of body and soul (or ph. who is flesh and spirit) IEph 7:2. s. JOtt, D. Bezeichnung Christi als ἰατρός in d. urchristl. Literatur: Der Katholik 90, 1910, 457f; AvHarnack, Mission4 I 1923, 129ff; RAC I 720–25. On medical practice in the Gr-Rom. world s. ANRW II Principat 37, 1–3, 93–96.—B. 308. DELG s.v. ἰάομαι. M-M. SEG XXXIX, 1804. TW.

Hom Hom , VIII b.c.—List 5

  1. cp. = compare, freq. in ref. to citation fr. ancient texts

pass. pass. = passive (either of grammatical form or of passive experience); also used in reference to literary portion=passage

Plut Plut , I–II a.d.—List 5

Stob Stob , V a.d.—List 5

  1. H. = FHort, s. W-H.—List 1, beg.

Ox Ox = POxy, but refers to fragments of documents esp. relating to the lexical data base—List 1

ASyn. ASyn. = KAland, Synopsis Quattuor Evangeliorum13—Lists 1, 6

GTh GTh = Gospel of Thomas in Coptic or in Oxy. documents—Lists 1

Dio Chrys Dio Chrys , I–II a.d.—List 5

Sb Sb = Sammelbuch griechischer Urkunden aus Aegypten—Lists 3, 4

v.l. v.l. = varia lectio (variant reading)

PStras PStras = Griechische Papyrus der kaiserlichen Universitäts- und Landesbibliothek zu Strassburg—List 4

Diod S Diod S , I b.c.—List 5

  1. ed. = edited (by), edition

Danker, Benefactor Danker, Benefactor = FD., Benefactor: Epigraphic Study—List 6

Heraclid Heraclid , perieg., III–II b.c.—List 5

Fgm. Fgm. = fragment, fragmentary

Strabo Strabo , I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

ins Ins, ins = Inscription, Inschrift, inscription(s). Without a period, esp. in lists, as at the beginning of entries; the capitalized form is used in titles. In conjunction with literary works this abbr. refers to the title or description of contents.

ad loc. ad loc. = ad locum (to or at the place under consideration)

Eur Eur , V b.c.—List 5

Nauck Nauck = AN., s. TGF—List 5

Aesop Aesop = a calque for a variety of lit. associated with the name of Aesop—List 5

Papias Papias , early Christian writer, II a.d.—List 1

AcPl Ha AcPl Ha = Acts of Paul, PHamb—List 1

IEph IEph = Ignatius to the Ephesians—List 1

Diog. L. Diog. L. = Diogenes Laertius, III a.d.—List 5

schol. schol. = scholion, scholia, scholiast

Pla Pla , V–IV b.c.; s. also Ps.-Plato—List 5

Philo Philo = P. of Alexandria, I b.c.–I a.d.—List 5

ZNW ZNW = Zeitschrift für die ntl. Wissenschaft—List 6

Aristoph Aristoph , V–IV b.c.—List 5

Lycophron Lycophron , trag., IV–III b.c.—List 5

Simplicius Simplicius , VI a.d.—List 5

Epict Epict , various works, I–II a.d.—List 5

Ael. Aristid. Ael. Aristid. = Aelius Aristides, II a.d.—List 5

D D = Didache, except that in a list of manuscripts or as textual variant D refers to Codex Bezae—List 1

Dg Dg = Letter of Diognetus, attributed to an unidentifiable apologist; II a.d.—List 1

RAC RAC = Reallexikon für Antike u. Christentum—List 6

Gr-Rom. Gr-Rom. = Greco-Roman (gener. in contrast to Israelite/Christian tradition)

ANRW ANRW = Aufstieg und Niedergang der Römischen Welt—List 6

  1. B. = CDBuck, A Dictionary of Selected Synonyms—List 6

DELG DELG = PChantraine, Dictionnaire étymologique de la langue grecque—List 6

s.v. s.v. = sub voce (under the word, look up the word)

M-M M-M = JMoulton/GMilligan, Vocabulary of Greek Testament—Lists 4, 6

SEG SEG = Supplementum Epigraphicum Graecum—List 3

TW TW = Theologisches Wörterbuch zum NT; tr. GBromiley, Theological Dictionary of the NT—List 6

Arndt, William ; Danker, Frederick W. ; Bauer, Walter: A Greek-English Lexicon of the New Testament and Other Early Christian Literature. 3rd ed. Chicago : University of Chicago Press, 2000, S. 465
